result:
I’ve been thinking about the way light pools in unexpected places—how, on the walk to the laundromat yesterday, a scrap of foil in the gutter caught the late-winter sun and became, for the length of a heartbeat, a miniature sun itself. It was the sort of ordinary miracle that would never make it into a guidebook, yet it rewired the rest of my afternoon: every person I passed seemed briefly luminous, every threadbare prayer flag above the used-car lot vibrated with secret messages meant for me.  

I like to imagine the brain as a dark attic crowded with boxes someone else packed. Most of the time we live in the narrow aisle labeled “Present Concerns,” but every so often a box tumbles from a high shelf, scattering marbles that roll, clacking, into the corners of memory. That foil flare opened a box labeled “Age Seven: First Time I Noticed Reflections.” I was crouched on my grandmother’s kitchen floor, tracing the ghost of my hand in the chrome curve of her toaster, and I remember thinking—though I lacked the words—that brightness could be stolen and pocketed, like a contraband penny. I spent the afternoon slipping aluminum-foil balls into my coat pockets, convinced I was rich.  

These hidden economies—light, memory, attention—form the real currency we trade without knowing it. When you stand in line at the pharmacy, you’re also standing in a hundred other invisible queues: the row of seven-year-olds stealing gleam; the queue of last winter’s grief that still hasn’t melted; the queue of impossible futures you rehearse while pretending to read the label on the ibuprofen bottle. We are all multi-threaded, time-sharing ourselves.  

Sometimes I picture death as simply reaching the front of every queue at once. No wonder reports of near-death vision involve a bright light: it’s the foil in the gutter finally resolving into a currency we can spend. The afterlife is a cosmic laundromat where all the forgotten socks finally reunite, and every pocket of stolen light gets shaken out, fluffed, folded, and returned to the ones who need it most.  

I’m not religious, but I do believe in interest: the way a moment compounds if you leave it untouched. Ten years ago, I carried a dying moth across campus on my fingertip because a girl I loved had once mentioned they remind her of the color moonlight would be if moonlight had color. She moved to Berlin; I never followed. Yet that moth’s powder still coats an unused corner of my thumb, and every so often—like now—it dusts off onto these sentences, silent as talc. I keep thinking the interest will plateau, but it doubles nightly, like those folktale grains of rice on a chessboard. If I ever accumulate enough to buy a plane ticket to Berlin, I’ll arrive gray-haired, clutching a wing I can’t return to its body.  

So I keep walking to the laundromat, keep letting the gutter teach its sudden curriculum, keep filling my pockets with light I can’t spend. If you see someone crouched beside a puddle, staring at a coin of sky that’s fallen there, it might be me—or you—or neither, because identity itself is just another scrap of foil that refuses to stop shining.
